perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall restrict any and all circulation of Confidential Information to a limited number of its employees on a "need to know basis" for the exclusive purpose of reviewing the Confidential Information for the Purpose of this Agreement. Recipient acknowledges that all information is provided "AS IS" and without any warranty, whether express or implied, as to its accuracy or completeness, non-infringement or use for particular purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall restrict any and all circulation of Confidential Information to a limited number of its employees on a "need to know basis" for the exclusive purpose of reviewing the Confidential Information for the Purpose of this Agreement. In WITNESS THEREOF, FormFactor and Recipient have executed this Agreement as of the Effective Date. Recipient acknowledges that all information is provided "AS IS" and without any warranty, whether express or implied, as to its accuracy or completeness, non-infringement or use for particular purpose.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the "AS IS" disclaimer to after the signature block makes it less clear that the disclaimer was agreed to by both parties at the time of signing. This is because the signature block implies agreement only to the terms preceding it. It raises a question of whether the disclaimer is part of the agreed-upon terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-316 governs the exclusion or modification of warranties. While this agreement relates to confidential information rather than goods, the principle that disclaimers of warranty must be conspicuous and clearly understood at the time of agreement is relevant. Moving the disclaimer after the signature block weakens the argument that the Recipient knowingly agreed to the "AS IS" provision, potentially rendering the disclaimer less enforceable under general contract law principles analogous to UCC 2-316 and raising legal doubt about contract law principles.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2-202 ) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2)Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof." (3)Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remed
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Uniform Commercial Code | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Uniform Commercial Code The Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) is a comprehensive set of laws governing commercial transactions, adopted in some form by every state and the District of Columbia. It aims to provide uniformity and consistency across states’ commercial laws . The UCC covers various aspects of commerce , including the sale and lease of goods , negotiable instruments , secured transactions , as well as on other categories related to banks and commercial transactions . See also: Commercial Law [Last reviewed in July of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities business law commercial law wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The altered text moves the "AS IS" disclaimer after the signature block, which creates a structural flaw that weakens the enforceability of the disclaimer. UCC § 2-316(3)(a), as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that "unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like 'as is'." By positioning the disclaimer after the signature, it raises doubt about whether the Recipient agreed to this provision at the time of signing, potentially undermining its validity. This creates a contradiction under the Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction definition, where the placement of a required disclosure affects its enforceability.
